---
title: "Parameter space"
description: "Extract posterior draws with spread_draws() and plot them"
status: stable
---

`spread_draws()` is the entry point: it pulls posterior draws out of an ArviZ `DataTree` into a tidy Polars `DataFrame` — one row per `chain × draw × coordinate`, each variable a column. Hand that frame to any plotting library; `tidydraws` does no plotting itself.

## The string spec

A spec names a variable and, in brackets, the dimensions to spread over. The bracketed names must match coordinate names in the `DataTree`.

| Spec | Meaning | Rows |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `"sigma"` | scalar parameter | `chain × draw` |
| `"beta[groups]"` | one-dimensional array | `chain × draw × groups` |
| `"gamma[time, groups]"` | nested two-dimensional array | `chain × draw × time × groups` |

Request several variables in one call and `tidydraws` joins them: variables sharing a dimension are inner-joined; a scalar is broadcast across an array's dimensions.

## Setup

A small hierarchical posterior — 4 chains × 500 draws, 4 groups, and a 2-D `gamma[time, groups]`. In practice this `DataTree` comes straight from PyMC or Stan.

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import arviz as az
import tidydraws as td
from xarray import DataArray
from lets_plot import *
LetsPlot.setup_html()

np.random.seed(7)
chains, draws, n_groups, n_time = 4, 500, 4, 3
beta = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.2, (chains, draws, n_groups))
intercept = np.random.normal(0.0, 0.5, (chains, draws, n_groups))
gamma = np.random.normal(0.5, 0.15, (chains, draws, n_time, n_groups))
sigma = np.random.normal(0.8, 0.1, (chains, draws))

dt = az.from_dict(
    {"posterior": {
        "beta": DataArray(beta, dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
        "intercept": DataArray(intercept, dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
        "gamma": DataArray(gamma, dims=["chain", "draw", "time", "groups"]),
        "sigma": DataArray(sigma, dims=["chain", "draw"]),
    }},
    coords={
        "groups": [f"g{i}" for i in range(n_groups)],
        "time": [f"t{i}" for i in range(n_time)],
    },
    dims={"beta": ["groups"], "intercept": ["groups"], "gamma": ["time", "groups"]},
)
```

The whole call site is one line:

```{python}
beta_df = td.spread_draws(dt, "beta[groups]", "intercept[groups]")
beta_df.head()
```

## Gallery

### Posterior density by group

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Posterior density of `beta` by group."
(
    ggplot(beta_df.to_pandas(), aes("beta", fill="groups"))
    + geom_density(alpha=0.5)
    + labs(x="beta", y="density", fill="group",
           title="Posterior distribution of beta by group")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

### Forest plot (89% credible interval)

Summarise per group with Polars quantiles, then `geom_pointrange`.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Forest plot of `beta` by group (89% credible interval)."
summary = (
    beta_df.group_by("groups")
    .agg(
        pl.col("beta").quantile(0.055).alias("lower"),
        pl.col("beta").median().alias("median"),
        pl.col("beta").quantile(0.945).alias("upper"),
    )
    .sort("groups")
)

(
    ggplot(summary.to_pandas(), aes("groups", "median"))
    + geom_pointrange(aes(ymin="lower", ymax="upper"), size=0.8)
    + geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#888888")
    + labs(x="group", y="beta", title="Posterior beta by group (89% CrI)")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

### Parameters against each other (cross-dim join)

Request `beta[groups]` and the scalar `sigma` together: `sigma` is broadcast onto every `beta[groups]` row, so you can colour one by the other directly.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Posterior `beta` by group, coloured by the draw's `sigma` — the cross-dimension join in action."
mixed = td.spread_draws(dt, "beta[groups]", "sigma")

(
    ggplot(mixed.to_pandas(), aes("groups", "beta"))
    + geom_jitter(aes(color="sigma"), width=0.15, alpha=0.15, size=0.8)
    + scale_color_gradient(low="steelblue", high="firebrick")
    + labs(x="group", y="beta", color="sigma",
           title="beta draws coloured by sigma")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

### Nested two-dimensional parameter

`gamma[time, groups]` spreads over both dims at once — facet to see the full grid.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Posterior density of `gamma` over `time × group`."
gamma_df = td.spread_draws(dt, "gamma[time, groups]")

(
    ggplot(gamma_df.to_pandas(), aes("gamma", fill="groups"))
    + geom_density(alpha=0.5)
    + facet_wrap(facets="time", ncol=3)
    + labs(x="gamma", y="density", fill="group",
           title="Posterior gamma by time x group")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Same data, different backend

`tidydraws` returns a Polars `DataFrame`; `.to_pandas()` is the only bridge. The
same summary frame renders identically with [plotnine](https://plotnine.org/)
as with lets-plot — swap the import, keep the geoms.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "The forest plot above, rendered with plotnine."
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_pointrange, geom_hline, labs, theme_minimal,
)

(
    ggplot(summary.to_pandas(), aes("groups", "median"))
    + geom_pointrange(aes(ymin="lower", ymax="upper"), size=0.8)
    + geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#888888")
    + labs(x="group", y="beta", title="Posterior beta by group (plotnine)")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```
:::

Next, join prediction draws to covariates with [`add_epred_draws()`](add_epred_draws.qmd).
